```
 │   Stage 1    │    │   Stage 2    │    │   Stage 3    │    │   Stage 4    │
 │  Clone Repo  │───>│  AI Planner  │───>│  AI Content  │───>│    HTML      │
 │              │    │  (plan.json) │    │  Generator   │    │  Renderer    │
 └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
       │                    │                    │                    │
       v                    v                    v                    v
   temp directory       plan.json          cache/pages/*.md     site/*.html
```

The entire pipeline is triggered by a single API call:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo_url": "https://github.com/org/repo"}'
```

---

## Stage 1: Clone Repository

The pipeline begins by cloning the target repository to a temporary directory on disk. This gives the AI full filesystem access to every file in the project.

### How It Works

docsfy performs a **shallow clone** (`--depth 1`) to minimize disk usage and clone time. Only the latest commit on the default branch is fetched — full history is not needed for documentation generation.

```bash
git clone --depth 1 <repo_url> <temp_directory>
```

### Repository Access

Both public and private repositories are supported:

| URL Format | Access Type | Authentication |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| `https://github.com/org/repo` | Public | None required |
| `https://github.com/org/repo` | Private | System git credentials |
| `git@github.com:org/repo.git` | Private (SSH) | SSH keys on the host |

Private repository access relies on the git credentials available in the container's environment. When running in Docker, SSH keys or credential helpers can be mounted into the container via volumes.

> **Note:** The cloned repository is placed in a temporary directory that persists throughout the pipeline. It is used as the working directory for both the AI Planner (Stage 2) and AI Content Generator (Stage 3) stages, giving the AI CLI tools direct filesystem access to explore the source code.

### Commit SHA Tracking

After cloning, docsfy records the current commit SHA in its SQLite database. This SHA is used later for [incremental updates](#incremental-updates) — when a re-generation is requested, docsfy compares the stored SHA against the latest commit to determine whether documentation needs to be refreshed.

---

## Stage 2: AI Planner

The second stage analyzes the repository and produces a structured documentation plan. An AI CLI tool explores the codebase — reading source files, configs, tests, and READMEs — then outputs a `plan.json` file that defines the pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy of the final documentation site.

### How It Works

The AI CLI is invoked as a subprocess with its working directory set to the cloned repository:

```python
subprocess.run(cmd, input=prompt, capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=repo_path)
```

This is wrapped for async execution:

```python
await asyncio.to_thread(subprocess.run, ...)
```

The prompt instructs the AI to explore the repository and produce a JSON documentation plan. The AI has full access to read any file in the repository and decides the optimal documentation structure based on what it finds.

### Provider-Specific Invocation

The exact command varies by AI provider:

| Provider | Command |
|----------|---------|
| Claude | `claude --model <model> --dangerously-skip-permissions -p` |
| Gemini | `gemini --model <model> --yolo` |
| Cursor | `agent --force --model <model> --print --workspace <path>` |

> **Note:** Cursor uses a `--workspace` flag instead of subprocess `cwd` to specify the repository path. This is handled internally by the `uses_own_cwd` flag in the provider configuration.

The provider configuration is defined as a frozen dataclass:

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProviderConfig:
    binary: str
    build_cmd: Callable
    uses_own_cwd: bool = False
```

### The plan.json Output

The AI produces a `plan.json` file that defines the complete documentation structure. This file is the contract between the planner and all subsequent stages. It contains:

- **Pages** — each page with a slug, title, and description of what it should cover
- **Sections** — logical groupings of pages (e.g., "Getting Started", "API Reference")
- **Navigation hierarchy** — the order and nesting of pages in the sidebar

A typical `plan.json` structure looks like:

```json
{
  "project_name": "my-project",
  "sections": [
    {
      "title": "Getting Started",
      "pages": [
        {
          "slug": "introduction",
          "title": "Introduction",
          "description": "Overview of the project, its purpose, and key features"
        },
        {
          "slug": "installation",
          "title": "Installation",
          "description": "How to install and set up the project"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "title": "Architecture",
      "pages": [
        {
          "slug": "architecture-overview",
          "title": "Architecture Overview",
          "description": "High-level system design and component interactions"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

The `plan.json` is saved to the project's storage directory at `/data/projects/{project-name}/plan.json` and serves as the source of truth for both content generation and HTML rendering.

### JSON Response Parsing

AI CLI tools don't always return clean JSON. docsfy uses a multi-strategy extraction approach to reliably parse the AI output:

1. **Direct JSON parse** — attempt `json.loads()` on the raw output
2. **Brace-matching** — scan for the outermost `{...}` JSON object in the output
3. **Markdown code block extraction** — extract JSON from `` ```json ... ``` `` fenced blocks
4. **Regex fallback** — last-resort recovery using pattern matching

This layered approach ensures robust parsing regardless of whether the AI wraps its response in explanatory text, markdown formatting, or other artifacts.

> **Tip:** If you encounter persistent JSON parsing failures, check the AI CLI's raw output. Some models add conversational text before or after the JSON. The multi-strategy parser handles most cases, but unusual formatting may require adjusting the prompt.

---

## Stage 3: AI Content Generator

With the documentation plan in hand, the third stage generates the actual content. For every page defined in `plan.json`, the AI CLI is invoked with a prompt that instructs it to write comprehensive markdown documentation for that specific topic.

### How It Works

Each page is generated independently:

1. Read the page definition from `plan.json` (title, slug, description)
2. Construct a prompt that references the page topic and instructs the AI to explore the codebase as needed
3. Invoke the AI CLI with `cwd` set to the cloned repository
4. Parse the markdown output
5. Cache the result to disk

### Concurrent Generation

Pages are generated concurrently using Python's async capabilities with semaphore-limited concurrency. This prevents overwhelming the AI CLI or the system while still generating multiple pages in parallel:

```python
# Conceptual flow — pages generated concurrently with bounded parallelism
semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

async def generate_page(page):
    async with semaphore:
        success, content = await run_ai_cli(prompt, cwd=repo_path)
        if success:
            save_to_cache(page.slug, content)

await asyncio.gather(*[generate_page(p) for p in plan.pages])
```

This concurrent approach significantly reduces total generation time for repositories with many documentation pages.

### Cached Output

Generated markdown files are cached on disk at:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/cache/pages/*.md
```

Each page's markdown is saved using its slug as the filename (e.g., `introduction.md`, `api-reference.md`). This cache serves two purposes:

1. **Incremental updates** — unchanged pages don't need to be regenerated
2. **Debugging** — the raw AI-generated markdown can be inspected before rendering

### AI CLI Timeout

Content generation is subject to a configurable timeout. Since complex pages may require the AI to explore many files, the default timeout is generous:

| Setting | Default | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` | `60` | Timeout in minutes per AI CLI invocation |

The return type from each AI invocation is a `tuple[bool, str]` — a success flag and the output content. Failed generations are logged and the page is skipped in the final output.

> **Warning:** Long timeouts mean a generation request can take significant time to complete, especially for large repositories with many planned pages. Monitor the project status via `GET /api/status` to track progress.

### Availability Check

Before starting the content generation pipeline, docsfy runs a lightweight availability check against the configured AI provider. A simple "Hi" prompt is sent to verify the CLI tool is installed, accessible, and properly authenticated. This fast-fails the pipeline with a clear error rather than waiting for a timeout on the first real prompt.

---

## Stage 4: HTML Renderer

The final stage transforms the AI-generated markdown and the `plan.json` navigation structure into a polished, fully-featured static HTML documentation site.

### How It Works

The renderer combines three inputs:

1. **`plan.json`** — defines navigation structure and page ordering
2. **Cached markdown files** — the content from Stage 3
3. **Jinja2 templates with bundled assets** — the visual theme and interactive features

The output is a self-contained static site written to:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/site/
```

### Output Structure

```
site/
├── index.html              # Landing page / first page
├── introduction.html       # One HTML file per documentation page
├── installation.html
├── api-reference.html
├── ...
├── assets/
│   ├── style.css           # Complete visual theme
│   ├── search.js           # Client-side full-text search
│   ├── theme-toggle.js     # Dark/light mode switching
│   └── highlight.js        # Code syntax highlighting
└── search-index.json       # Pre-built search index
```

### Features

The rendered HTML site includes a rich set of features out of the box:

| Feature | Implementation |
|---------|---------------|
| **Sidebar navigation** | Generated from `plan.json` sections and pages |
| **Dark/light theme** | Client-side toggle with `theme-toggle.js` |
| **Full-text search** | Client-side via lunr.js with `search-index.json` |
| **Syntax highlighting** | highlight.js for fenced code blocks |
| **Responsive design** | Mobile-friendly layout via CSS |
| **Card layouts** | Visual cards for feature overviews |
| **Callout boxes** | Note, warning, and info callouts |

### Templating

docsfy uses **Jinja2** for HTML templating. Templates are bundled with the application and define the page shell — header, sidebar, content area, and footer. The markdown content is converted to HTML using the Python `markdown` library and injected into the Jinja2 page template.

### Search Index

During rendering, docsfy builds a `search-index.json` file that indexes the content of all pages. This powers the client-side search feature — users can search across the entire documentation site without a backend server, making the static output fully self-contained.

---

## Incremental Updates

docsfy supports incremental updates to avoid regenerating an entire documentation site when only part of the repository has changed. The process works as follows:

1. **SHA comparison** — On re-generation, docsfy fetches the latest commit SHA and compares it against the stored SHA in SQLite
2. **Plan re-evaluation** — If the SHA has changed, the AI Planner (Stage 2) runs again to check whether the documentation structure needs to change
3. **Selective regeneration** — Only pages whose content may be affected by the changes are regenerated
4. **Cache reuse** — Unchanged pages are served from the markdown cache at `cache/pages/*.md`

```
Re-generate Request
       │
       v
  Compare commit SHA
       │
  ┌────┴────┐
  │ Changed │  No ──> Return existing site
  └────┬────┘
       │ Yes
       v
  Re-run AI Planner
       │
  ┌────┴──────────┐
  │ Plan changed? │  No ──> Regenerate affected pages only
  └────┬──────────┘
       │ Yes
       v
  Regenerate all affected pages
       │
       v
  Re-render HTML site
```

> **Tip:** Incremental updates significantly reduce generation time for active repositories. The first generation builds the full documentation; subsequent runs only update what has changed.

---

## Storage Layout

The complete filesystem layout for a generated project ties all four stages together:

```
/data/
├── docsfy.db                              # SQLite: project metadata, status, SHA
└── projects/
    └── {project-name}/
        ├── plan.json                      # Stage 2 output: documentation structure
        ├── cache/
        │   └── pages/
        │       ├── introduction.md        # Stage 3 output: AI-generated markdown
        │       ├── installation.md
        │       └── api-reference.md
        └── site/                          # Stage 4 output: rendered HTML
            ├── index.html
            ├── introduction.html
            ├── installation.html
            ├── api-reference.html
            ├── assets/
            │   ├── style.css
            │   ├── search.js
            │   ├── theme-toggle.js
            │   └── highlight.js
            └── search-index.json
```

The SQLite database tracks project-level metadata:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Project name | Derived from the repository name |
| Repository URL | The source GitHub URL |
| Status | `generating`, `ready`, or `error` |
| Last generated | Timestamp of the last successful generation |
| Last commit SHA | Used for incremental update comparison |
| Generation history | Logs from each generation run |

---

## Configuration

The pipeline behavior is controlled via environment variables:

```bash
# AI provider selection
AI_PROVIDER=claude          # Options: claude, gemini, cursor
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]  # Model ID passed to the AI CLI
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60           # Timeout per AI invocation (minutes)
```

### Provider Authentication

Each AI provider requires its own credentials:

**Claude (via API key):**
```bash
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
```

**Claude (via Vertex AI):**
```bash
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
CLOUD_ML_REGION=us-east5
ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=my-gcp-project
```

**Gemini:**
```bash
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

**Cursor:**
```bash
CURSOR_API_KEY=...
```

> **Warning:** AI CLI tools are installed unpinned (always the latest version) inside the container. While this ensures access to the newest features, it means pipeline behavior may change between container rebuilds. Pin your Docker image tags in production for reproducible builds.

---

## Error Handling

Each stage can fail independently, and docsfy handles failures at every level:

| Stage | Failure Mode | Behavior |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| Clone | Invalid URL, auth failure, network error | Pipeline aborts, project status set to `error` |
| AI Planner | AI CLI unavailable, timeout, unparseable JSON | Pipeline aborts, project status set to `error` |
| AI Content | Timeout on individual page | Page skipped, other pages continue |
| AI Content | AI CLI crash | Page skipped with logged error |
| HTML Renderer | Template error, missing pages | Pipeline aborts, project status set to `error` |

The availability check before content generation catches the most common failure — a misconfigured or unauthenticated AI CLI — before any significant work is done.


---

# AI CLI Integration

Source: ai-cli-integration.html

# AI CLI Integration

docsfy delegates all intelligence — repository analysis, documentation planning, and content generation — to external AI CLI tools running as subprocesses. This page explains the provider abstraction layer, how commands are built and executed, how AI responses are parsed, and how timeouts protect the pipeline from hung processes.

## Overview

Rather than embedding AI SDKs or calling HTTP APIs directly, docsfy shells out to locally-installed AI CLI binaries. This approach offers several advantages:

- **No SDK dependencies** — the AI tools manage their own authentication, model routing, and API versioning
- **Provider portability** — switching from Claude to Gemini or Cursor requires changing a single environment variable
- **Full repository context** — each CLI tool runs with `cwd` set to the cloned repository, giving it native file exploration capabilities

The integration layer follows the same provider pattern used in [jenkins-job-insight](https://github.com/), keeping the abstraction consistent across projects in the ecosystem.

## The ProviderConfig Pattern

At the core of the integration is a frozen dataclass that encapsulates everything docsfy needs to know about a given AI CLI tool:

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProviderConfig:
    binary: str
    build_cmd: Callable
    uses_own_cwd: bool = False
```

### Field Reference

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `binary` | `str` | Name of the CLI executable (e.g., `"claude"`, `"gemini"`, `"agent"`) |
| `build_cmd` | `Callable` | Function that constructs the full command-line argument list given a model name and optional workspace path |
| `uses_own_cwd` | `bool` | Whether the provider handles working directory via its own flag (e.g., `--workspace`) rather than subprocess `cwd`. Defaults to `False` |

> **Note:** The dataclass uses `frozen=True`, making provider configurations immutable after construction. This prevents accidental mutation during concurrent page generation where multiple tasks share the same config.

### Provider Definitions

Each supported provider has a corresponding `ProviderConfig` instance. The `build_cmd` callable encapsulates the provider-specific CLI flags:

```python
# Claude Code
ProviderConfig(
    binary="claude",
    build_cmd=lambda model, _: [
        "claude", "--model", model, "--dangerously-skip-permissions", "-p"
    ],
)

# Gemini CLI
ProviderConfig(
    binary="gemini",
    build_cmd=lambda model, _: [
        "gemini", "--model", model, "--yolo"
    ],
)

# Cursor Agent
ProviderConfig(
    binary="agent",
    build_cmd=lambda model, workspace: [
        "agent", "--force", "--model", model, "--print", "--workspace", workspace
    ],
    uses_own_cwd=True,
)
```

### Provider Command Summary

| Provider | Binary | Command | CWD Handling |
|----------|--------|---------|-------------|
| Claude | `claude` | `claude --model <model> --dangerously-skip-permissions -p` | subprocess `cwd` = repo path |
| Gemini | `gemini` | `gemini --model <model> --yolo` | subprocess `cwd` = repo path |
| Cursor | `agent` | `agent --force --model <model> --print --workspace <path>` | `--workspace` flag, `uses_own_cwd=True` |

> **Warning:** The `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag (Claude) and `--yolo` flag (Gemini) disable interactive permission prompts. These flags are required because docsfy runs AI tools non-interactively in a subprocess where no user is present to approve file reads. Only use docsfy in trusted environments, such as containers with cloned repositories.

### Working Directory Strategies

docsfy uses two strategies for giving the AI tool access to the repository, determined by the `uses_own_cwd` flag:

**Strategy 1: Subprocess `cwd` (default)**

For Claude and Gemini, the subprocess itself is launched with `cwd` pointing to the cloned repository. The AI tool inherits this working directory and can explore files relative to it.

```python
subprocess.run(cmd, cwd=repo_path, input=prompt, capture_output=True, text=True)
```

**Strategy 2: Provider-managed workspace (`uses_own_cwd=True`)**

For Cursor Agent, the workspace path is passed explicitly via the `--workspace` flag. The subprocess `cwd` is not set to the repo path since the tool manages its own working directory.

```python
# Cursor builds the workspace into its command
cmd = ["agent", "--force", "--model", model, "--print", "--workspace", repo_path]
subprocess.run(cmd, input=prompt, capture_output=True, text=True)
```

## Subprocess Execution

### Core Invocation

All AI CLI calls flow through a single execution function that wraps `subprocess.run` with async support:

```python
subprocess.run(cmd, input=prompt, capture_output=True, text=True)
```

Key aspects of the invocation:

- **Prompt delivery via stdin** — the `input=prompt` parameter pipes the prompt text to the CLI tool's standard input, avoiding shell escaping issues with complex prompts
- **Output capture** — `capture_output=True` captures both stdout (the AI response) and stderr (diagnostic output)
- **Text mode** — `text=True` ensures string I/O rather than bytes

### Async Wrapping

Since `subprocess.run` is a blocking call, docsfy wraps it with `asyncio.to_thread` to prevent blocking the FastAPI event loop:

```python
result = await asyncio.to_thread(subprocess.run, cmd, input=prompt, capture_output=True, text=True)
```

This is critical during Stage 3 (content generation), where multiple pages are generated concurrently using async tasks with semaphore-limited concurrency.

### Return Type

The execution function returns a `tuple[bool, str]`:

```python
tuple[bool, str]  # (success, output)
```

- `success` — `True` if the subprocess exited with return code 0
- `output` — the stdout content from the AI tool (the generated text or JSON)

> **Tip:** Check stderr in addition to the return code when debugging failed invocations. AI CLI tools often print diagnostic information, authentication errors, or rate-limit messages to stderr.

### Availability Check

Before starting a full generation pipeline, docsfy performs a lightweight availability check by sending a simple "Hi" prompt to the configured AI CLI tool:

```python
# Verify the AI CLI is installed and authenticated
success, output = await run_ai_cli(provider_config, model, prompt="Hi", repo_path="/tmp")
if not success:
    raise RuntimeError(f"AI CLI '{provider_config.binary}' is not available")
```

This catches common issues early:
- CLI binary not installed or not on `$PATH`
- Missing or expired authentication credentials
- Model not available for the configured provider

## JSON Response Parsing

AI CLI tools return free-form text, but docsfy needs structured JSON from certain stages — most critically, the `plan.json` output from Stage 2 (AI Planner). Since LLMs don't always produce clean JSON, docsfy uses a multi-strategy extraction approach with four fallback levels.

### Strategy 1: Direct JSON Parse

The simplest case — attempt to parse the entire output as valid JSON:

```python
import json

try:
    result = json.loads(output)
except json.JSONDecodeError:
    # Fall through to next strategy
    pass
```

This succeeds when the AI returns nothing but a well-formed JSON object with no surrounding text.

### Strategy 2: Brace-Matching Extraction

When the AI includes explanatory text before or after the JSON, brace-matching locates the outermost `{...}` object:

```python
# Find the first '{' and match it to its closing '}'
start = output.index('{')
# Walk forward, tracking brace depth, to find the matching close brace
```

This handles responses like:

```
Here's the documentation plan:

{"pages": [...], "navigation": [...]}

Let me know if you'd like any changes.
```

### Strategy 3: Markdown Code Block Extraction

LLMs frequently wrap JSON in markdown code fences. This strategy strips the fencing:

````
```json
{
  "pages": [
    {"title": "Getting Started", "slug": "getting-started"}
  ]
}
```
````

The parser extracts content between the opening `` ```json `` (or `` ``` ``) and the closing `` ``` `` markers, then parses the inner text as JSON.

### Strategy 4: Regex Recovery

As a last resort, regex patterns attempt to extract and reconstruct JSON fragments from heavily malformed output. This handles edge cases like:

- Truncated JSON (missing closing braces)
- Multiple JSON fragments in the output
- JSON interleaved with commentary

> **Note:** The parsing strategies are applied in order. As soon as one succeeds, the result is returned. This layered approach means clean AI output is parsed instantly (Strategy 1), while progressively messier output is handled by later strategies.

### When Parsing Is Used

| Pipeline Stage | Expected Output | Parsing Required |
|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Stage 2: AI Planner | `plan.json` structure | Yes — must extract structured JSON |
| Stage 3: AI Content Generator | Markdown text | No — raw text output used directly |
| Availability check | Any text response | No — only checks success/failure |

## Timeout Handling

Long-running AI CLI processes are a real risk — a complex repository might take significant time to analyze, or a tool might hang indefinitely. docsfy uses a configurable timeout to guard against this.

### Configuration

The timeout is controlled via the `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` environment variable:

```bash
# In .env or environment
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60  # timeout in minutes
```

| Setting | Default | Unit | Description |
|---------|---------|------|-------------|
| `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` | `60` | minutes | Maximum time allowed for each AI CLI subprocess call |

> **Tip:** For large repositories with many files, consider increasing the timeout beyond the default 60 minutes. Conversely, for small projects or during development, a shorter timeout (e.g., 10-15 minutes) can help catch issues faster.

### Timeout Scope

The timeout applies independently to each AI CLI subprocess invocation, not to the overall pipeline:

```
Pipeline execution:
├── Stage 2: AI Planner ................ ← AI_CLI_TIMEOUT applies
└── Stage 3: AI Content Generator
    ├── Page 1 ......................... ← AI_CLI_TIMEOUT applies
    ├── Page 2 ......................... ← AI_CLI_TIMEOUT applies
    └── Page N ......................... ← AI_CLI_TIMEOUT applies
```

This means:
- A single page generation can take up to the full timeout duration
- Total pipeline time can exceed `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` when generating multiple pages
- Each concurrent page generation task has its own independent timeout

> **Warning:** With the default 60-minute timeout and many pages generating concurrently, the total generation time for a large project could be substantial. Monitor generation status via the `GET /api/status` endpoint.

## Default Configuration

The complete set of AI CLI integration defaults:

| Setting | Default Value | Environment Variable |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Provider | `claude` | `AI_PROVIDER` |
| Model | `claude-opus-4-6[1m]` | `AI_MODEL` |
| Timeout | `60` minutes | `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` |

### Example `.env` Configuration

```bash
# Use Claude (default)
AI_PROVIDER=claude
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60

# Claude authentication — Option 1: API Key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# Claude authentication — Option 2: Vertex AI
# CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
# CLOUD_ML_REGION=us-east5
# ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=my-project
```

```bash
# Use Gemini instead
AI_PROVIDER=gemini
AI_MODEL=gemini-2.5-pro
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=90
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

```bash
# Use Cursor instead
AI_PROVIDER=cursor
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60
CURSOR_API_KEY=...
```

## Provider Selection at Runtime

The active provider is resolved from the `AI_PROVIDER` environment variable and looked up in a registry of `ProviderConfig` instances. This happens once at the start of each generation pipeline:

```
POST /api/generate
    │
    ▼
Read AI_PROVIDER env var ──► Look up ProviderConfig
    │
    ▼
Availability check (send "Hi" prompt)
    │
    ▼
Run pipeline with selected provider
```

> **Note:** Changing `AI_PROVIDER` or `AI_MODEL` takes effect on the next generation request. In-progress generations continue using the provider that was active when they started.

## Concurrency Model

During Stage 3, multiple pages are generated concurrently to reduce total pipeline time. The concurrency model works as follows:

```python
# Semaphore limits how many AI CLI subprocesses run simultaneously
semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

async def generate_page(page_info):
    async with semaphore:
        return await asyncio.to_thread(
            subprocess.run, cmd, input=prompt,
            capture_output=True, text=True
        )

# Launch all page generation tasks concurrently
tasks = [generate_page(page) for page in plan["pages"]]
results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
```

The semaphore prevents overloading the system by limiting the number of simultaneous AI CLI subprocesses. Each subprocess still has its own independent timeout via `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT`.


---

# Storage & Database

Source: storage-and-database.html

# Storage & Database

docsfy uses a two-layer storage architecture: a **SQLite database** for project metadata and tracking, and a **filesystem directory tree** for cached markdown content, rendered HTML sites, and search indexes. Both live under a single `/data` mount point for simple container deployment.

## Storage Overview

```
/data/
  docsfy.db                          # SQLite database (project metadata)
  projects/                          # Filesystem storage (generated content)
    {project-name}/
      plan.json                      # Documentation structure from AI Planner
      cache/
        pages/*.md                   # AI-generated markdown (cached)
      site/                          # Final rendered static HTML site
        index.html
        *.html
        assets/
          style.css
          search.js
          theme-toggle.js
          highlight.js
        search-index.json            # Client-side search index
```

All storage is rooted at `/data`, which is mapped as a Docker volume:

```yaml
# docker-compose.yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
```

> **Tip:** Since all persistent state lives under `/data`, backing up this single directory captures everything — the database, cached content, and rendered sites.

## SQLite Database

### Location

The database file lives at `/data/docsfy.db`. docsfy accesses it asynchronously via `aiosqlite`, which is well-suited for the FastAPI async request lifecycle.

### Schema

The database stores **project metadata only** — it does not store page content or rendered HTML. The tracked fields are:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Project name | Unique identifier derived from the repository |
| Repository URL | Source GitHub repository (SSH or HTTPS) |
| Status | Current generation state: `generating`, `ready`, or `error` |
| Last generated | Timestamp of the most recent successful generation |
| Last commit SHA | HEAD commit SHA from the last generation run |
| Generation history | Logs from previous generation runs |

### Project Status Lifecycle

A project transitions through these statuses during generation:

```
POST /api/generate
       │
       v
  ┌──────────┐     success     ┌───────┐
  │generating │ ──────────────> │ ready │
  └──────────┘                  └───────┘
       │
       │ failure
       v
  ┌───────┐
  │ error │
  └───────┘
```

- **`generating`** — Pipeline is actively running (clone, plan, content generation, render).
- **`ready`** — Site is built and available for serving at `GET /docs/{project}/`.
- **`error`** — Generation failed; logs are stored for debugging.

### Incremental Update Tracking

The database plays a central role in docsfy's incremental update strategy. When a project is re-generated:

1. The stored **last commit SHA** is compared against the repository's current HEAD.
2. If the SHA has changed, the AI Planner re-runs to check if the documentation structure changed.
3. Only pages whose source content may be affected are regenerated.
4. If the plan structure is unchanged and only specific files changed, only the relevant pages are rebuilt.

> **Note:** The commit SHA comparison happens before any AI invocation, making the "nothing changed" case fast and free of AI costs.

## Filesystem Layout

### Per-Project Directory

Each project gets its own directory at `/data/projects/{project-name}/` containing three distinct areas:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/
  plan.json
  cache/
    pages/*.md
  site/
    ...
```

### plan.json — Documentation Structure

The `plan.json` file is the output of **Stage 2 (AI Planner)** and defines the entire documentation structure: pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy.

This file is:
- Generated by the AI CLI after analyzing the full repository
- Used by the content generator to know which pages to write
- Used by the HTML renderer to build sidebar navigation
- Persisted for incremental update comparisons (to detect if the doc structure changed)

### cache/pages/ — Markdown Cache

```
cache/
  pages/
    getting-started.md
    api-reference.md
    configuration.md
    ...
```

The `cache/pages/` directory holds the **AI-generated markdown** files produced in **Stage 3 (AI Content Generator)**. Each page defined in `plan.json` gets a corresponding `.md` file.

These cached markdown files serve two purposes:

1. **Input for rendering** — The HTML renderer converts these into the final static site.
2. **Incremental update cache** — On re-generation, unchanged pages can be skipped if their source material hasn't changed, avoiding unnecessary AI invocations.

> **Warning:** Manually editing files in `cache/pages/` is not recommended. They will be overwritten on the next generation run. To customize output, adjust AI prompts or post-process the rendered site instead.

### site/ — Rendered HTML Output

```
site/
  index.html
  getting-started.html
  api-reference.html
  configuration.html
  assets/
    style.css
    search.js
    theme-toggle.js
    highlight.js
  search-index.json
```

The `site/` directory contains the **final static HTML output** from **Stage 4 (HTML Renderer)**. This is a fully self-contained static site that can be:

- **Served directly** by docsfy at `GET /docs/{project}/{path}`
- **Downloaded** as a `.tar.gz` archive via `GET /api/projects/{name}/download` for self-hosting

The renderer uses **Jinja2 templates** with bundled CSS and JavaScript assets to produce a polished documentation site with:

- Sidebar navigation (generated from `plan.json`)
- Dark/light theme toggle
- Client-side search
- Code syntax highlighting (via highlight.js)
- Responsive design
- Callout boxes (note, warning, info)
- Card layouts

## Search Index

### How It Works

docsfy uses **client-side search** powered by lunr.js (or similar). The search index is generated at build time during the HTML rendering stage and served as a static JSON file.

### search-index.json

The search index lives at:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/site/search-index.json
```

This JSON file is built from the markdown content of all pages during Stage 4. It contains pre-processed, tokenized content that the client-side search library can query without any server-side infrastructure.

### search.js

The corresponding search JavaScript is bundled as a static asset:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/site/assets/search.js
```

This script loads `search-index.json` on the client side and provides instant search results as the user types — no server roundtrips required.

> **Tip:** Because search is entirely client-side, downloaded/self-hosted sites get full search functionality with no additional setup.

## Docker Volume Mapping

The recommended deployment maps `/data` as a bind mount or named volume:

```yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
      - ~/.config/gcloud:/home/appuser/.config/gcloud:ro
      - ./cursor:/home/appuser/.config/cursor
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
```

The container runs as a non-root user (`appuser`) for OpenShift compatibility. The `/data` directory must be writable by this user.

> **Warning:** If you use a bind mount (`./data:/data`), ensure the host directory exists and has appropriate permissions before starting the container.

## Storage Management

### Removing a Project

Use the API to cleanly remove a project and all its associated storage:

```
DELETE /api/projects/{name}
```

This removes:
- The project record from the SQLite database
- The entire `/data/projects/{name}/` directory tree (plan, cache, and site)

### Disk Space Considerations

Each generated project consumes disk space across three areas:

| Area | Contents | Relative Size |
|------|----------|---------------|
| `plan.json` | Documentation structure | Small (a few KB) |
| `cache/pages/` | AI-generated markdown | Moderate (depends on page count) |
| `site/` | Rendered HTML + assets + search index | Largest (HTML + bundled JS/CSS) |

The SQLite database (`docsfy.db`) itself remains small, as it stores only metadata — not page content.

> **Tip:** To reclaim space without losing the ability to re-render, you can delete the `site/` directory for a project. The cached markdown and plan can be used to regenerate the HTML without re-invoking the AI.


---

# HTML Renderer

Source: html-renderer.html

# HTML Renderer

The HTML Renderer is Stage 4 of the docsfy generation pipeline. It transforms AI-generated Markdown pages and the `plan.json` navigation structure into a polished, self-contained static HTML documentation site.

## Overview

The renderer takes two inputs:

1. **`plan.json`** — the documentation structure produced by the AI Planner (Stage 2), defining pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy
2. **Markdown files** — per-page content generated by the AI Content Generator (Stage 3), cached at `/data/projects/{name}/cache/pages/*.md`

It produces a complete static site at `/data/projects/{name}/site/` that can be served directly by the docsfy API or downloaded as a `.tar.gz` archive for self-hosting.

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/site/
  index.html
  *.html
  assets/
    style.css
    search.js
    theme-toggle.js
    highlight.js
  search-index.json
```

## Jinja2 Templating

The renderer uses [Jinja2](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/) as its templating engine to convert Markdown content into fully-structured HTML pages. Each page defined in `plan.json` is rendered through a base template that provides consistent layout, navigation, and asset loading.

### Template Context

When rendering a page, the following context variables are passed to the Jinja2 template:

| Variable | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `page_title` | `str` | Title of the current page |
| `page_content` | `str` | Rendered HTML from Markdown source |
| `project_name` | `str` | Name of the documentation project |
| `navigation` | `list[dict]` | Sidebar navigation tree from `plan.json` |
| `current_page` | `str` | Slug/path of the active page (for highlighting) |
| `pages` | `list[dict]` | All pages metadata for search index generation |

### Markdown Processing

Markdown content is converted to HTML using the Python `markdown` library before being injected into the template. The renderer processes several extended Markdown features:

- **Fenced code blocks** — converted to `<pre><code>` elements with language classes for syntax highlighting
- **Callout boxes** — blockquote patterns for note, warning, and info callouts are transformed into styled `<div>` elements
- **Card layouts** — structured content blocks rendered as visual cards
- **Tables** — GitHub-flavored Markdown tables with responsive wrappers

#### Callout Syntax

The renderer recognizes three callout types in Markdown source:

```markdown
> **Note:** This provides additional context or helpful information.

> **Warning:** This highlights potential issues or breaking changes.

> **Info:** This offers supplementary details about the topic.
```

These are rendered as styled callout boxes with distinct colors and icons for each type.

### Example: Rendered Page Structure

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en" data-theme="light">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{{ page_title }} - {{ project_name }}</title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="assets/style.css">
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="assets/highlight.css">
</head>
<body>
    <nav class="sidebar">
        <!-- Sidebar navigation -->
    </nav>
    <main class="content">
        <header class="top-bar">
            <!-- Search bar and theme toggle -->
        </header>
        <article class="page-content">
            {{ page_content }}
        </article>
    </main>
    <script src="assets/highlight.js"></script>
    <script src="assets/search.js"></script>
    <script src="assets/theme-toggle.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

## Bundled CSS/JS Assets

The renderer bundles all frontend assets into the `assets/` directory of the generated site. This ensures the documentation is fully self-contained with no external CDN dependencies — the entire site works offline.

### Asset Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `style.css` | Complete site styling including layout, typography, responsive design, dark/light themes |
| `search.js` | Client-side full-text search engine and UI |
| `theme-toggle.js` | Dark/light theme switching with localStorage persistence |
| `highlight.js` | Code syntax highlighting via [highlight.js](https://highlightjs.org/) |
| `search-index.json` | Pre-built search index generated at render time |

Assets are copied from the docsfy package's bundled templates into each project's site directory during rendering, ensuring every generated site is independent.

## Sidebar Navigation

The sidebar provides hierarchical navigation generated from the `plan.json` structure. It supports nested sections and highlights the currently active page.

### Navigation Structure from `plan.json`

The `plan.json` file produced by the AI Planner defines the navigation hierarchy:

```json
{
  "project_name": "my-project",
  "pages": [
    {
      "slug": "introduction",
      "title": "Introduction",
      "description": "Project overview and getting started",
      "section": "Getting Started"
    },
    {
      "slug": "installation",
      "title": "Installation",
      "description": "How to install and configure",
      "section": "Getting Started"
    },
    {
      "slug": "api-reference",
      "title": "API Reference",
      "description": "Complete API documentation",
      "section": "Reference"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Sidebar Rendering

Pages are grouped by their `section` field into collapsible navigation groups. The renderer:

1. Parses `plan.json` to extract the page list
2. Groups pages by `section` to create a two-level hierarchy
3. Renders each group as a collapsible section with its pages listed beneath
4. Marks the current page with an `active` class for visual highlighting

```html
<nav class="sidebar">
    <div class="sidebar-header">
        <h2>{{ project_name }}</h2>
    </div>
    <ul class="nav-tree">
        {% for section, pages in navigation %}
        <li class="nav-section">
            <span class="section-title">{{ section }}</span>
            <ul>
                {% for page in pages %}
                <li class="{% if page.slug == current_page %}active{% endif %}">
                    <a href="{{ page.slug }}.html">{{ page.title }}</a>
                </li>
                {% endfor %}
            </ul>
        </li>
        {% endfor %}
    </ul>
</nav>
```

### Mobile Sidebar

On smaller viewports, the sidebar collapses into a hamburger menu overlay. A toggle button appears in the top bar to open and close the sidebar. See [Responsive Design](#responsive-design) for breakpoint details.

## Dark/Light Theme Toggle

The renderer includes a theme toggle that allows users to switch between dark and light modes. Theme preference is persisted in the browser's `localStorage`.

### How It Works

The theme system uses a `data-theme` attribute on the `<html>` element:

```html
<html lang="en" data-theme="light">
```

The `theme-toggle.js` script manages the toggle behavior:

1. **On page load** — checks `localStorage` for a saved preference, falls back to the system preference via `prefers-color-scheme`, and defaults to `light`
2. **On toggle click** — switches the `data-theme` attribute between `light` and `dark` and saves the choice to `localStorage`
3. **Across pages** — the saved preference is applied immediately on every page load to prevent flash of wrong theme (FOWT)

### CSS Theme Variables

The theme is implemented using CSS custom properties that change based on the `data-theme` attribute:

```css
:root,
[data-theme="light"] {
    --bg-primary: #ffffff;
    --bg-secondary: #f8f9fa;
    --bg-sidebar: #f1f3f5;
    --text-primary: #212529;
    --text-secondary: #495057;
    --border-color: #dee2e6;
    --link-color: #228be6;
    --code-bg: #f1f3f5;
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
    --bg-primary: #1a1b1e;
    --bg-secondary: #25262b;
    --bg-sidebar: #18191c;
    --text-primary: #c1c2c5;
    --text-secondary: #909296;
    --border-color: #373a40;
    --link-color: #4dabf7;
    --code-bg: #2c2e33;
}
```

All site styles reference these variables rather than hard-coded color values, ensuring seamless theme switching without page reloads.

### Toggle Button

The theme toggle renders as a sun/moon icon button in the top bar:

```html
<button class="theme-toggle" aria-label="Toggle dark mode">
    <span class="icon-sun">☀️</span>
    <span class="icon-moon">🌙</span>
</button>
```

> **Tip:** The toggle respects the user's operating system preference on first visit. If a user has their OS set to dark mode, the documentation site will load in dark mode automatically.

## Client-Side Search

The renderer generates a pre-built search index at render time and bundles a client-side search engine. This enables instant full-text search without any server-side component — ideal for static hosting.

### Search Index Generation

During rendering, the renderer builds `search-index.json` by extracting searchable content from each page:

```python
search_entries = []
for page in plan["pages"]:
    search_entries.append({
        "slug": page["slug"],
        "title": page["title"],
        "section": page["section"],
        "body": strip_html_tags(rendered_content),
    })
```

The resulting `search-index.json` contains the full text of every page, stripped of HTML tags, alongside metadata for display in search results.

### Search UI

The search interface consists of a search input in the top bar and a results dropdown:

```html
<div class="search-container">
    <input type="text" class="search-input"
           placeholder="Search documentation..."
           aria-label="Search documentation">
    <div class="search-results" hidden>
        <!-- Results populated by search.js -->
    </div>
</div>
```

### How Search Works

The `search.js` script provides client-side full-text search:

1. **Index loading** — on first search interaction, `search-index.json` is fetched and parsed
2. **Query matching** — user input is matched against page titles, section names, and body text
3. **Result ranking** — matches are ranked by relevance (title matches rank higher than body matches)
4. **Result display** — matching pages are shown in a dropdown with title, section, and a text snippet with the matching term highlighted

> **Note:** The search index is loaded lazily on first interaction to avoid impacting initial page load performance.

### Keyboard Navigation

The search results support keyboard navigation:

- `Ctrl+K` or `/` — focus the search input
- `↑` / `↓` — navigate through results
- `Enter` — navigate to the selected result
- `Escape` — close the results dropdown

## Code Syntax Highlighting

The renderer uses [highlight.js](https://highlightjs.org/) for automatic syntax highlighting of fenced code blocks. Highlighting runs client-side after page load.

### Supported Languages

highlight.js provides syntax highlighting for a wide range of languages out of the box, including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, Rust, Java, C/C++, Shell/Bash, JSON, YAML, HTML, CSS, SQL, and many more.

### Markdown Code Blocks

Fenced code blocks in Markdown source are converted to properly-classed HTML elements:

````markdown
```python
from docsfy.renderer import render_site

render_site(project_name="my-project", plan=plan, pages=pages)
```
````

This renders as:

```html
<pre><code class="language-python">from docsfy.renderer import render_site

render_site(project_name="my-project", plan=plan, pages=pages)
</code></pre>
```

The `highlight.js` script then detects the `language-*` class and applies syntax highlighting automatically on page load:

```javascript
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () {
    document.querySelectorAll("pre code").forEach(function (block) {
        hljs.highlightElement(block);
    });
});
```

### Theme-Aware Highlighting

Code block colors adapt to the current theme. The highlight.js theme CSS uses the same CSS custom properties as the rest of the site:

```css
pre {
    background: var(--code-bg);
    border: 1px solid var(--border-color);
    border-radius: 6px;
    padding: 1rem;
    overflow-x: auto;
}

code {
    font-family: 'SF Mono', 'Fira Code', 'Cascadia Code', monospace;
    font-size: 0.875rem;
    line-height: 1.5;
}
```

> **Tip:** If a code block has no language specified, highlight.js will attempt automatic language detection. For best results, always specify the language in your fenced code blocks.

## Responsive Design

The generated documentation sites are fully responsive, adapting to desktops, tablets, and mobile devices using CSS media queries and a flexible layout system.

### Layout Structure

The site uses a two-column layout with a fixed sidebar and scrollable main content area:

```css
body {
    display: flex;
    min-height: 100vh;
}

.sidebar {
    width: 280px;
    position: fixed;
    height: 100vh;
    overflow-y: auto;
}

.content {
    margin-left: 280px;
    flex: 1;
    max-width: 900px;
    padding: 2rem;
}
```

### Breakpoints

The renderer uses the following responsive breakpoints:

| Breakpoint | Width | Behavior |
|------------|-------|----------|
| Desktop | `≥ 1024px` | Full two-column layout with visible sidebar |
| Tablet | `768px – 1023px` | Narrower sidebar, adjusted content margins |
| Mobile | `< 768px` | Sidebar hidden behind hamburger menu, full-width content |

### Mobile Adaptations

On mobile viewports (`< 768px`), the following adaptations are applied:

```css
@media (max-width: 768px) {
    .sidebar {
        transform: translateX(-100%);
        position: fixed;
        z-index: 1000;
        transition: transform 0.3s ease;
    }

    .sidebar.open {
        transform: translateX(0);
    }

    .content {
        margin-left: 0;
        padding: 1rem;
    }

    .hamburger-btn {
        display: block;
    }

    pre {
        font-size: 0.8rem;
    }

    table {
        display: block;
        overflow-x: auto;
    }
}
```

Key mobile behaviors:

- **Sidebar** collapses into an off-canvas overlay toggled by a hamburger button
- **Code blocks** use smaller font sizes and allow horizontal scrolling
- **Tables** become horizontally scrollable within their container
- **Content** padding is reduced for more readable line lengths
- **Search** expands to full width

### Typography

The renderer applies a clean, readable typographic scale:

```css
body {
    font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto,
                 Oxygen, Ubuntu, Cantarell, sans-serif;
    font-size: 16px;
    line-height: 1.7;
    color: var(--text-primary);
}

h1 { font-size: 2rem; margin-top: 2rem; }
h2 { font-size: 1.5rem; margin-top: 1.75rem; }
h3 { font-size: 1.25rem; margin-top: 1.5rem; }
```

## Output Structure

After rendering completes, the full static site is written to `/data/projects/{name}/site/`. The output directory contains everything needed to host the documentation independently:

```
site/
├── index.html              # Landing page / first page in plan
├── introduction.html       # Individual page per plan entry
├── installation.html
├── api-reference.html
├── assets/
│   ├── style.css           # Complete site styles (dark + light themes)
│   ├── search.js           # Client-side search engine
│   ├── theme-toggle.js     # Theme switching logic
│   └── highlight.js        # Code syntax highlighting
└── search-index.json       # Pre-built search index
```

### Serving the Output

The generated site can be served in two ways:

1. **Via the docsfy API** — access at `GET /docs/{project}/{path}` directly from the running docsfy service
2. **Self-hosted** — download the site as a `.tar.gz` archive via `GET /api/projects/{name}/download` and serve it from any static file host (Nginx, Caddy, S3, GitHub Pages, etc.)

> **Note:** Because all assets are bundled and there are no external dependencies, the generated site works entirely offline once downloaded. No CDN or internet connection is required.

### Incremental Re-rendering

When a project is regenerated incrementally (see [Incremental Updates](../plans/2026-03-04-docsfy-design.md)), the renderer only re-renders pages whose Markdown source has changed. The search index is always fully rebuilt to ensure consistency. Unchanged pages retain their existing HTML output.


---

# POST /api/generate

Source: api-generate.html

# POST /api/generate

Start documentation generation for a GitHub repository. This endpoint triggers the full generation pipeline — cloning the repository, planning the documentation structure with AI, generating page content, and rendering a polished static HTML site.

## Endpoint

```
POST /api/generate
```

## Request Body

Send a JSON object containing the GitHub repository URL to generate documentation for.

```json
{
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repository"
}
```

### Parameters

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `repo_url` | `string` | Yes | The GitHub repository URL. Supports both HTTPS and SSH formats. |

### Supported URL Formats

Both public and private repositories are supported:

```bash
# HTTPS (public or private)
https://github.com/owner/repository

# SSH (private repos using system git credentials)
git@github.com:owner/repository.git
```

> **Note:** Private repository access relies on system git credentials configured in the container environment. See [Configuration](/configuration) for details on mounting credentials.

## Response

A successful request returns `200 OK` with the project metadata, including its current generation status.

```json
{
  "name": "repository",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repository",
  "status": "generating",
  "last_generated": null,
  "last_commit_sha": null
}
```

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | The project name, derived from the repository name. |
| `repo_url` | `string` | The repository URL that was submitted. |
| `status` | `string` | Current generation status. One of `generating`, `ready`, or `error`. |
| `last_generated` | `string \| null` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the last successful generation, or `null` if not yet complete. |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string \| null` | The commit SHA used for the most recent generation, or `null` if not yet complete. |

### Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generating` | The documentation pipeline is currently running. |
| `ready` | Documentation has been successfully generated and is available to view or download. |
| `error` | Generation failed. Check project details for error logs. |

## Example

### cURL

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo_url": "https://github.com/myk-org/docsfy"}'
```

### Python (requests)

```python
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api/generate",
    json={"repo_url": "https://github.com/myk-org/docsfy"}
)

project = response.json()
print(f"Project '{project['name']}' status: {project['status']}")
```

### Python (httpx async)

```python
import httpx

async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
    response = await client.post(
        "http://localhost:8000/api/generate",
        json={"repo_url": "https://github.com/myk-org/docsfy"}
    )
    project = response.json()
```

## Generation Pipeline

When a request is accepted, docsfy runs four sequential stages in the background:

### Stage 1: Clone Repository

The repository is shallow-cloned (`--depth 1`) to a temporary directory. This minimizes disk usage and clone time while providing full access to the current source tree.

### Stage 2: AI Planner

The configured AI CLI analyzes the entire repository and produces a `plan.json` file containing the documentation structure — pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy.

```python
# The AI CLI runs with cwd set to the cloned repo directory
# Provider commands vary by configuration:

# Claude
claude --model <model> --dangerously-skip-permissions -p

# Gemini
gemini --model <model> --yolo

# Cursor
agent --force --model <model> --print --workspace <path>
```

### Stage 3: AI Content Generator

For each page defined in `plan.json`, the AI CLI runs with full access to the cloned repository. Pages are generated concurrently using async execution with semaphore-limited concurrency. Generated markdown files are cached at `/data/projects/{name}/cache/pages/*.md`.

### Stage 4: HTML Renderer

Markdown pages and `plan.json` are converted into a polished static HTML site using Jinja2 templates with bundled CSS/JS assets. The final site includes:

- Sidebar navigation
- Dark/light theme toggle
- Client-side search (via lunr.js)
- Code syntax highlighting (via highlight.js)
- Callout boxes (note, warning, info)
- Responsive design

Output is written to `/data/projects/{name}/site/`.

> **Tip:** Once generation completes with status `ready`, view the docs at `GET /docs/{project}/` or download them as a `.tar.gz` archive via `GET /api/projects/{name}/download`.

## Incremental Updates

Calling `POST /api/generate` for a repository that has already been generated triggers an incremental update:

1. The current commit SHA is compared against the stored SHA from the last generation.
2. If the repository has changed, the AI Planner re-evaluates the documentation structure.
3. Only pages affected by the changes are regenerated — unchanged pages use cached markdown.
4. The HTML renderer rebuilds the full site with updated content.

> **Note:** If the repository has not changed since the last generation, the endpoint returns the existing project metadata without re-running the pipeline.

## Error Handling

| HTTP Status | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `200` | Generation started successfully (or project already exists). |
| `422` | Invalid request body — missing or malformed `repo_url`. |
| `500` | Internal server error during pipeline initialization. |

If generation fails during any pipeline stage, the project status is set to `error`. Retrieve error details using `GET /api/projects/{name}`.

```json
{
  "name": "repository",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repository",
  "status": "error",
  "last_generated": null,
  "last_commit_sha": null
}
```

> **Warning:** Generation is a long-running process that involves cloning a repository and making multiple AI CLI calls. The `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` environment variable (default: `60` minutes) controls the maximum time allowed for each AI invocation.

## Related Configuration

The AI provider and model used during generation are controlled by environment variables:

```bash
# AI Configuration
AI_PROVIDER=claude          # Options: claude, gemini, cursor
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]  # Model identifier for the selected provider
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60           # Timeout per AI invocation in minutes
```

See the [Configuration](/configuration) page for the full list of environment variables and provider setup instructions.

## Related Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `GET` | `/api/status` | List all projects and their generation status. |
| `GET` | `/api/projects/{name}` | Get project details including generation logs. |
| `DELETE` | `/api/projects/{name}` | Remove a project and its generated documentation. |
| `GET` | `/api/projects/{name}/download` | Download the generated site as a `.tar.gz` archive. |
| `GET` | `/docs/{project}/{path}` | Serve the generated static HTML documentation. |


---

# GET /api/status

Source: api-status.html

# GET /api/status

List all projects and their current documentation generation status.

## Overview

The `GET /api/status` endpoint returns a summary of every project registered in docsfy along with its current generation state. Use this endpoint to monitor ongoing generation jobs, verify completed builds, or detect failures across all your documentation projects at a glance.

## Request

```
GET /api/status
```

No query parameters, request body, or authentication are required.

### Example Request

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/status
```

```python
import httpx

response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api/status")
projects = response.json()
```

## Response

Returns a JSON array of project objects. Each object contains the project name, its source repository, and the current generation status.

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Unique project identifier derived from the repository name |
| `repo_url` | `string` | GitHub repository URL used for generation |
| `status` | `string` | Current generation status: `generating`, `ready`, or `error` |
| `last_generated` | `string \| null` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the last successful generation, or `null` if never completed |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string \| null` | Git commit SHA from the most recent generation run |

### Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generating` | The documentation pipeline is actively running. The project is being cloned, planned, or rendered. |
| `ready` | Generation completed successfully. The static HTML site is available to serve or download. |
| `error` | Generation failed at some stage. Check project details for logs. |

### Example Response

```json
[
  {
    "name": "my-api",
    "repo_url": "https://github.com/acme/my-api",
    "status": "ready",
    "last_generated": "2026-03-04T14:32:10Z",
    "last_commit_sha": "a1b2c3d4e5f6"
  },
  {
    "name": "frontend-sdk",
    "repo_url": "https://github.com/acme/frontend-sdk",
    "status": "generating",
    "last_generated": null,
    "last_commit_sha": null
  },
  {
    "name": "data-pipeline",
    "repo_url": "https://github.com/acme/data-pipeline",
    "status": "error",
    "last_generated": "2026-03-03T09:15:42Z",
    "last_commit_sha": "f6e5d4c3b2a1"
  }
]
```

When no projects have been registered yet, the endpoint returns an empty array:

```json
[]
```

## Status Lifecycle

A project's status follows this lifecycle through the four-stage [generation pipeline](/api/generate):

```
POST /api/generate
       │
       v
  ┌────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
  │ generating  │────>│  Clone → Plan → Content → HTML  │
  └────────────┘     └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
                                    │
                       ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                       v                         v
                 ┌──────────┐             ┌──────────┐
                 │  ready   │             │  error   │
                 └──────────┘             └──────────┘
```

1. **`generating`** — Set immediately when a `POST /api/generate` request is accepted. The project stays in this state while the pipeline progresses through cloning, AI planning, AI content generation, and HTML rendering.

2. **`ready`** — Set when the HTML renderer completes successfully and the static site is written to `/data/projects/{name}/site/`. The docs are now available at `GET /docs/{name}/` and can be downloaded via `GET /api/projects/{name}/download`.

3. **`error`** — Set if any pipeline stage fails. Use `GET /api/projects/{name}` to retrieve detailed error logs and determine which stage caused the failure.

> **Note:** Re-running `POST /api/generate` for a project in `error` or `ready` status resets it back to `generating` and starts a fresh pipeline run.

## Storage

Project status metadata is persisted in the SQLite database at `/data/docsfy.db`. This ensures status information survives service restarts.

```
/data/docsfy.db          # SQLite: project name, repo URL, status,
                         #         last_generated, last_commit_sha,
                         #         generation history, logs
```

The filesystem stores the generated artifacts separately:

```
/data/projects/{name}/
  ├── plan.json          # Documentation structure from AI planner
  ├── cache/
  │   └── pages/*.md     # AI-generated markdown (cached)
  └── site/              # Final rendered HTML (available when status is "ready")
```

## Usage Patterns

### Polling for Generation Completion

After triggering a generation with `POST /api/generate`, poll `/api/status` to check when the project finishes:

```python
import httpx
import time

def wait_for_project(base_url: str, project_name: str, timeout: int = 600):
    """Poll until a project leaves the 'generating' state."""
    deadline = time.time() + timeout
    while time.time() < deadline:
        response = httpx.get(f"{base_url}/api/status")
        response.raise_for_status()
        for project in response.json():
            if project["name"] == project_name:
                if project["status"] != "generating":
                    return project
        time.sleep(5)
    raise TimeoutError(f"{project_name} still generating after {timeout}s")

result = wait_for_project("http://localhost:8000", "my-api")
print(f"Status: {result['status']}")
```

> **Tip:** For details on a specific project — including page listings, generation logs, and commit metadata — use `GET /api/projects/{name}` instead.

### Filtering by Status

The endpoint returns all projects. Filter client-side to find projects in a specific state:

```python
import httpx

response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api/status")
projects = response.json()

errored = [p for p in projects if p["status"] == "error"]
if errored:
    print(f"{len(errored)} project(s) failed generation:")
    for p in errored:
        print(f"  - {p['name']} ({p['repo_url']})")
```

### Health Monitoring with `jq`

```bash
# Count projects by status
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/status | \
  jq 'group_by(.status) | map({status: .[0].status, count: length})'

# List only projects currently generating
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/status | \
  jq '[.[] | select(.status == "generating")]'

# Get names of all failed projects
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/status | \
  jq -r '[.[] | select(.status == "error") | .name] | .[]'
```

## Related Endpoints

| Endpoint | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `POST /api/generate` | Start documentation generation for a repository |
| `GET /api/projects/{name}` | Get detailed project info including pages and logs |
| `DELETE /api/projects/{name}` | Remove a project and its generated docs |
| `GET /api/projects/{name}/download` | Download the generated site as a `.tar.gz` archive |
| `GET /docs/{project}/{path}` | Serve the generated static HTML documentation |

> **Warning:** The `/api/status` endpoint reflects the live state of the database. If a generation process is forcefully interrupted (e.g., the container is restarted), a project may remain stuck in `generating` status. Re-submit the project via `POST /api/generate` to restart the pipeline.


---

# GET /api/projects/{name}

Source: api-projects.html

# GET /api/projects/{name}

Retrieve detailed information about a specific documentation project, including its current status, the last generated timestamp, the commit SHA used for generation, and the full list of generated pages.

## Endpoint

```
GET /api/projects/{name}
```

## Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Yes | The project name. This matches the directory name under `/data/projects/`. |

## Response

### 200 OK

Returns the full project details.

```json
{
  "name": "docsfy",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/username/docsfy.git",
  "status": "ready",
  "last_generated": "2026-03-04T14:32:10Z",
  "last_commit_sha": "c6db255a9f1e4b8d7c3a2e6f0b1d5c8a4e7f9b2d",
  "pages": [
    {
      "title": "Introduction",
      "path": "index.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "Getting Started",
      "path": "getting-started.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "API Reference",
      "path": "api-reference.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "Configuration",
      "path": "configuration.html"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | The project name. |
| `repo_url` | `string` | The source GitHub repository URL (SSH or HTTPS). |
| `status` | `string` | Current project status. One of `generating`, `ready`, or `error`. |
| `last_generated` | `string` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the most recent successful generation. `null` if the project has never completed generation. |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string` | The full Git commit SHA that was used for the last generation. Used internally for [incremental updates](#incremental-updates). `null` if no generation has completed. |
| `pages` | `array` | List of pages in the generated documentation site, derived from the project's `plan.json`. Empty array if generation is still in progress. |

### Project Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generating` | Documentation generation is currently in progress. The pipeline is running through clone, planning, content generation, or HTML rendering stages. |
| `ready` | Documentation has been successfully generated and is available for serving or download. |
| `error` | The most recent generation attempt failed. Check generation logs for details. |

### 404 Not Found

Returned when no project with the given name exists.

```json
{
  "detail": "Project not found: my-project"
}
```

## Examples

### Retrieve a project with completed documentation

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/projects/docsfy
```

```json
{
  "name": "docsfy",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/username/docsfy.git",
  "status": "ready",
  "last_generated": "2026-03-04T14:32:10Z",
  "last_commit_sha": "c6db255a9f1e4b8d7c3a2e6f0b1d5c8a4e7f9b2d",
  "pages": [
    {
      "title": "Introduction",
      "path": "index.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "Getting Started",
      "path": "getting-started.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "API Reference",
      "path": "api-reference.html"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Retrieve a project that is currently generating

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-library
```

```json
{
  "name": "my-library",
  "repo_url": "git@github.com:org/my-library.git",
  "status": "generating",
  "last_generated": null,
  "last_commit_sha": null,
  "pages": []
}
```

### Retrieve a project after a failed generation

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/projects/broken-repo
```

```json
{
  "name": "broken-repo",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/org/broken-repo.git",
  "status": "error",
  "last_generated": "2026-03-03T09:15:00Z",
  "last_commit_sha": "a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0",
  "pages": [
    {
      "title": "Introduction",
      "path": "index.html"
    }
  ]
}
```

> **Note:** When a project has `status: "error"`, the `last_generated` and `pages` fields reflect the last **successful** generation, not the failed attempt. If no generation has ever succeeded, `last_generated` will be `null` and `pages` will be empty.

### Handle a non-existent project

```bash
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:8000/api/projects/nonexistent
```

```
404
```

## Incremental Updates

The `last_commit_sha` field plays a key role in docsfy's incremental update strategy. When a project is re-generated via `POST /api/generate`, docsfy compares the current repository HEAD against the stored `last_commit_sha` to determine what has changed:

1. If the SHA matches, no regeneration is needed
2. If the SHA differs, the AI Planner re-evaluates the documentation structure
3. Only pages affected by the changes are regenerated, keeping cached pages intact

This means a project's `last_commit_sha` always reflects the repository state from which the current documentation was built.

> **Tip:** Poll this endpoint after triggering a generation with `POST /api/generate` to track when the `status` transitions from `generating` to `ready` or `error`.

## Storage Details

The data returned by this endpoint is assembled from two sources:

- **SQLite database** (`/data/docsfy.db`) — stores the project metadata: name, repo URL, status, last generated timestamp, last commit SHA, and generation history
- **Filesystem** (`/data/projects/{name}/plan.json`) — the AI-generated documentation plan that defines the page list, sections, and navigation hierarchy

The filesystem structure for a project looks like:

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/
  plan.json             # Doc structure from AI (pages, sections, navigation)
  cache/
    pages/*.md          # AI-generated markdown (cached for incremental updates)
  site/                 # Final rendered static HTML
    index.html
    *.html
    assets/
      style.css
      search.js
      theme-toggle.js
      highlight.js
    search-index.json
```

## Related Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `POST` | `/api/generate` | Start documentation generation for a repository |
| `GET` | `/api/status` | List all projects and their generation status |
| `DELETE` | `/api/projects/{name}` | Remove a project and its generated docs |
| `GET` | `/api/projects/{name}/download` | Download the generated site as a `.tar.gz` archive |
| `GET` | `/docs/{project}/{path}` | Serve the generated static HTML documentation |

> **Warning:** Calling `DELETE /api/projects/{name}` will permanently remove all generated documentation, cached markdown, and metadata for the project. This action cannot be undone.


---

# DELETE /api/projects/{name}

Source: api-projects-delete.html

# DELETE /api/projects/{name}

Remove a project and all its generated documentation from the server. This permanently deletes the project metadata from the SQLite database and removes all associated files from the filesystem, including cached markdown, rendered HTML, and the documentation plan.

## Endpoint

```
DELETE /api/projects/{name}
```

## Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Yes | The unique name of the project to delete. This is the project name assigned during generation via `POST /api/generate`. |

## Response

### 200 OK

The project and all associated documentation were successfully deleted.

```json
{
  "status": "deleted",
  "project": "my-project"
}
```

### 404 Not Found

The specified project does not exist.

```json
{
  "detail": "Project 'my-project' not found"
}
```

## Examples

### cURL

```bash
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project
```

### Python (requests)

```python
import requests

response = requests.delete("http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project")

if response.status_code == 200:
    print(f"Project deleted: {response.json()}")
elif response.status_code == 404:
    print(f"Project not found: {response.json()['detail']}")
```

### Python (httpx, async)

```python
import httpx

async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
    response = await client.delete("http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project")
    response.raise_for_status()
    print(response.json())
```

## What Gets Deleted

When a project is deleted, the following resources are permanently removed:

### Database Records

Project metadata is deleted from the SQLite database at `/data/docsfy.db`, including:

- Project name and repository URL
- Generation status (`generating` / `ready` / `error`)
- Last generated timestamp and commit SHA
- Generation history and logs

### Filesystem

The entire project directory at `/data/projects/{name}/` is removed, which includes:

```
/data/projects/{name}/
  plan.json               # documentation structure from AI planner
  cache/
    pages/*.md            # AI-generated markdown content
  site/                   # rendered static HTML site
    index.html
    *.html
    assets/
      style.css
      search.js
      theme-toggle.js
      highlight.js
    search-index.json
```

> **Warning:** This operation is irreversible. All generated documentation, cached markdown pages, and the AI-produced documentation plan will be permanently lost. To regenerate, you must submit a new `POST /api/generate` request, which will re-clone the repository and run the full AI generation pipeline from scratch.

## Behavior During Active Generation

If a project is currently in the `generating` state, the delete request will remove the project record and any partially written files. Any in-progress AI generation tasks for the project will be cancelled.

> **Note:** Check the project status with `GET /api/projects/{name}` before deleting to confirm whether generation is still in progress.

```bash
# Check project status before deleting
curl http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project

# Then delete
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project
```

## Served Documentation

Once a project is deleted, its documentation is immediately no longer accessible at the serving endpoint:

```
GET /docs/{name}/{path}
```

Any requests to the project's documentation pages will return a `404 Not Found` after deletion.

> **Tip:** If you need to preserve a copy of the generated documentation before deleting, download the site archive first using `GET /api/projects/{name}/download`, which provides the complete static HTML site as a `.tar.gz` file suitable for self-hosting.

```bash
# Download a backup before deleting
curl -o my-project-docs.tar.gz http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project/download

# Then safely delete
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project
```

## Re-generating After Deletion

After deleting a project, you can regenerate its documentation by submitting a new generation request:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repo"}'
```

> **Note:** Unlike incremental updates (which compare commit SHAs and regenerate only affected pages), a full generation after deletion runs the complete four-stage pipeline: repository cloning, AI planning, AI content generation, and HTML rendering.

## Related Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `POST` | `/api/generate` | Start documentation generation for a repository |
| `GET` | `/api/status` | List all projects and their generation status |
| `GET` | `/api/projects/{name}` | Get project details before deleting |
| `GET` | `/api/projects/{name}/download` | Download site archive as backup before deleting |


---

# GET /api/projects/{name}/download

Source: api-projects-download.html
